questions.stem: A registered nurse receives a new patient assignment at shift start. The RN reviews the five patients and determines that patient care tasks need to be distributed. Which of the following tasks can be appropriately delegated to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

options.text: Perform the admission assessment for a new patient with unstable angina
Develop the care plan for a patient with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes
Administer a scheduled dose of metoprolol 25 mg PO to a stable cardiac patient with a heart rate of 72 bpm
Educate the patient and family about a new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation

questions.rationale: LPNs/LVNs can administer medications (including PO medications) to stable patients within their scope of practice, following the five rights of medication administration. This is a routine, scheduled medication for a stable patient with an appropriate heart rate — appropriate for LPN delegation. Admission assessment requires comprehensive RN-level assessment skills. Care plan development requires RN clinical judgment and nursing diagnosis formulation. Patient/family education for a new diagnosis requires RN-level assessment of learning needs, teaching, and evaluation — this is an RN function.

distractorRationales.a: Perform the admission assessment for a new patient with unstable angina fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in Patient/family education for a new diagnosis requires RN-level assessment of learning needs, teaching. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The priority is Administer a scheduled dose of metoprolol 25 mg PO to a stable cardiac patient with a, not a step that delays stabilization of circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.b: This option makes Develop the care plan for a patient with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes the focus, but the stem cue is RN scope: initial assessment, care planning, teaching, evaluating unstable patients. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for circulation and perfusion support while circulation or perfusion progresses.
distractorRationales.d: Educate the patient and family about a new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by RN scope: initial assessment, care planning, teaching, evaluating unstable patients. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so Administer a scheduled dose of metoprolol 25 mg PO to a stable cardiac patient with a better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: LPN scope: administer medications to stable patients, perform routine procedures, collect data for stable conditions. RN scope: initial assessment, care planning, teaching, evaluating unstable patients, and tasks requiring clinical judgment. RNs cannot delegate assessment, teaching, or care planning.

questions.stem: A nurse reviews orders for a patient admitted for an elective knee MRI with IV contrast scheduled for tomorrow. The patient takes metformin 1000 mg BID for type 2 diabetes. Which nursing action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective
Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the ordering provider
Switch the patient to insulin therapy permanently before the procedure
Administer metformin with the morning meal on the day of the procedure and hold only for 24 hours post-contrast

questions.rationale: Metformin is renally cleared. IV iodinated contrast can cause contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN), acutely impairing renal function. If renal function is reduced by CIN, metformin accumulates and raises the risk of lactic acidosis — a rare but potentially fatal complication. Standard protocol: hold metformin on the day of contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then re-assess renal function (creatinine/eGFR) before resuming. Permanent insulin switch is not necessary. Administering on the day of the procedure violates the safety protocol. The provider should be notified to confirm the hold order.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in If renal function is reduced by CIN, metformin accumulates and raises the risk of lactic acidosis — a. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization and leave unstable glucose physiology unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Switch the patient to insulin therapy permanently before the procedure, but the stem cue is Standard protocol: hold metformin on the day of contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then re-assess renal. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: Administer metformin with the morning meal on the day of the procedure and hold only for is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by Check creatinine/eGFR before restarting. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Metformin + IV contrast: hold the day of and 48h after. Risk is lactic acidosis from metformin accumulation if contrast causes AKI. Check creatinine/eGFR before restarting. This applies to any iodinated contrast study.

questions.subcategory: Safety and Infection Control / Stable vs. Unstable
questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient 2 days post-abdominal surgery. The patient calls the nurse and states, 'I felt something pop in my stomach and I'm looking at my intestines.' The nurse enters the room and observes evisceration of abdominal contents. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Attempt to manually replace the intestines and apply a sterile dressing
Cover the eviscerated contents with sterile normal saline-soaked gauze, do not attempt to replace them, and call for help
Place the patient supine with legs straight, apply a dry sterile dressing, and activate the surgical team
Ask the patient to lie still, apply an abdominal binder to contain the bowel, and document the finding

questions.rationale: Wound evisceration is a surgical emergency. The priority is to protect the eviscerated tissue from desiccation and contamination. Cover the bowel immediately with sterile gauze moistened with normal saline (not dry gauze, which would desiccate tissue; not replaced, which risks contamination and injury). The patient should be kept NPO, positioned to reduce tension on the wound (often supine with knees slightly flexed), and the surgical team notified immediately for emergent return to the OR. Attempting to replace bowel or using dry dressings or binders can cause irreparable harm.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Attempt to manually replace the intestines and apply a sterile dressing is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Wound evisceration is a surgical emergency. Cover the eviscerated contents with sterile normal saline-soaked gauze, do not attempt to replace them, better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Place the patient supine with legs straight, apply a dry sterile dressing, and activate the surgical, without accounting for The priority is to protect the eviscerated tissue from desiccation and contamination. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.d: Ask the patient to lie still, apply an abdominal binder to contain the bowel, and document fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on The patient should be kept NPO, positioned to reduce tension on the wound (often supine with knees slightly. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Cover the eviscerated contents with sterile normal saline-soaked gauze, do not attempt to replace them,; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.

tags: Safety and Infection Control / Stable vs. Unstable
questions.takeaway: Evisceration: Cover with sterile saline-soaked gauze (NEVER replace), keep patient still, call surgeon STAT, keep NPO. Dry gauze and binders are contraindicated.

questions.stem: A nurse at a community health center is providing care for a homeless patient with chronic schizophrenia who has been non-adherent to antipsychotics and presents with decompensated psychosis. The patient refuses hospitalization. Which nursing approach reflects the ethical principle of justice and the public health model for vulnerable populations?

options.text: Immediately call law enforcement for involuntary commitment since the patient is homeless
Discharge the patient with a 30-day prescription and advise them to follow up in one month

Apply harm reduction principles: assess immediate safety, engage assertive community treatment (ACT), coordinate long-acting injectable antipsychotic if appropriate, and connect to housing and social services
Refuse care until the patient agrees to hospitalization to protect the healthcare team

questions.rationale: Justice in community health requires equitable care for vulnerable populations, who face structural barriers (housing instability, stigma, limited healthcare access) that increase health disparities. Harm reduction principles: meet patients where they are, reduce immediate harm without requiring abstinence/full adherence, minimize barriers to engagement. Assertive Community Treatment (ACT): multidisciplinary team providing intensive outreach, case management, and mental health services to individuals with severe mental illness who are not engaging with traditional services — evidence-based, reduces hospitalization and homelessness. Long-acting injectable antipsychotics address non-adherence barriers. Involuntary commitment requires imminent danger to self/others, not simply homelessness or non-adherence.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor pulls attention toward Immediately call law enforcement for involuntary commitment since the patient is homeless, and misses that Harm reduction principles: meet patients where they are, reduce immediate harm without requiring abstinence/full adherence, minimize barriers to. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The correct path is Apply harm reduction principles: assess immediate safety, engage assertive community treatment (ACT), coordinate long-acting injectable antipsychotic, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Discharge the patient with a 30-day prescription and advise them to follow up in one month is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, without accounting for Harm reduction principles: meet patients where they are, reduce immediate harm without requiring abstinence/full adherence, minimize barriers to. Apply harm reduction principles: assess immediate safety, engage assertive community treatment (ACT), coordinate long-acting injectable antipsychotic is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Refuse care until the patient agrees to hospitalization to protect the healthcare team is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Harm reduction principles: meet patients where they are, reduce immediate harm without requiring abstinence/full adherence, minimize barriers to. Apply harm reduction principles: assess immediate safety, engage assertive community treatment (ACT), coordinate long-acting injectable antipsychotic is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Homeless, mentally ill, non-adherent patient: apply harm reduction + Assertive Community Treatment (ACT); involuntary commitment requires imminent danger (not just homelessness); address social determinants of health.

questions.stem: During delivery, after the baby's head delivers, the anterior shoulder fails to deliver. The turtle sign is present (head retracts against the perineum). Which intervention is attempted FIRST?

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby down
McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure
Zavanelli maneuver: cephalic replacement followed by emergency cesarean
Perform an episiotomy to enlarge the vaginal opening

questions.rationale: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency. The McRoberts maneuver (sharply flexing maternal thighs onto the abdomen) flattens the lumbar lordosis and rotates the pubic symphysis superiorly, increasing the functional AP diameter of the pelvis. Combined with suprapubic pressure (pushing the anterior shoulder downward and laterally), this resolves approximately 90% of cases. Fundal pressure (A) is absolutely contraindicated — it worsens impaction of the shoulder. Zavanelli maneuver (C) is a last resort when all other maneuvers fail. Episiotomy (D) alone does not help shoulder dystocia as the obstruction is bony, not soft tissue.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Apply fundal pressure to push the baby down is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, but the stem cue is Fundal pressure (A) is absolutely contraindicated — it worsens impaction of the shoulder. McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Zavanelli maneuver: cephalic replacement followed by emergency cesarean, despite the clinical warning in Combined with suprapubic pressure (pushing the anterior shoulder downward and laterally), this resolves approximately 90% of cases. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Perform an episiotomy to enlarge the vaginal opening, without accounting for Shoulder dystocia: McRoberts + suprapubic pressure first. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so McRoberts maneuver: hyperflexion of maternal thighs against the abdomen with suprapubic pressure better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Shoulder dystocia: McRoberts + suprapubic pressure first; fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated — it worsens the impaction.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient undergoes repair of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. Post-operatively, the nurse notes urine output 10 mL/hr for 3 consecutive hours, lower extremity mottling, pain and weakness of both legs, and absent dorsalis pedis pulses bilaterally. MAP is 62 mmHg. Which complication BEST explains all findings?

options.text: Acute kidney injury from contrast nephropathy
Abdominal compartment syndrome with visceral and spinal cord ischemia
Bilateral femoral artery thrombosis from arterial catheter placement
Graft limb occlusion with distal ischemia and atheroembolism

questions.rationale: After large-volume resuscitation and abdominal surgery, abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS) can develop. Elevated intra-abdominal pressure compresses the renal veins and IVC (oliguria), decreases spinal cord perfusion (lower extremity weakness/paralysis), and causes visceral ischemia. Mottling and absent pedal pulses in combination with oliguria and leg weakness after AAA repair point to ACS causing multi-organ ischemia. Bladder pressure >20 mmHg with organ dysfunction confirms the diagnosis. Bilateral femoral thrombosis (C) would not explain oliguria. Contrast nephropathy (A) would not explain limb ischemia.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Acute kidney injury from contrast nephropathy, rather than acting on After large-volume resuscitation and abdominal surgery, abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS) can develop. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The priority is Abdominal compartment syndrome with visceral and spinal cord ischemia, not a step that delays stabilization of renal injury.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Bilateral femoral artery thrombosis from arterial catheter placement the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Elevated intra-abdominal pressure compresses the renal veins and IVC (oliguria), decreases spinal cord perfusion (lower extremity weakness/paralysis). Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for renal protection while renal injury progresses.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Graft limb occlusion with distal ischemia and atheroembolism is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, without accounting for Post-AAA oliguria + lower extremity ischemia + elevated bladder pressure = abdominal compartment syndrome. It is less defensible because Abdominal compartment syndrome with visceral and spinal cord ischemia targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Post-AAA oliguria + lower extremity ischemia + elevated bladder pressure = abdominal compartment syndrome. Measure bladder pressure.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed lithium about safety monitoring. Which statement by the patient indicates correct understanding of lithium toxicity prevention?

options.text: 'I should limit salt in my diet to prevent water retention since lithium can cause edema.'
'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea, vomiting, or excessive sweating.'
'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.'
'Ibuprofen is safe to take for headaches while on lithium.'

questions.rationale: Lithium is excreted by the kidney and competes with sodium for reabsorption. When sodium levels drop (from low-sodium diet, diarrhea, vomiting, sweating, diuretics), the kidney retains lithium — causing toxicity. Consistent sodium and fluid intake, and monitoring for dehydration causes, are essential safety measures. Low-sodium diet (A) increases lithium retention and toxicity risk — the opposite of safe. Abrupt discontinuation (C) causes rebound mania. NSAIDs like ibuprofen (D) decrease renal lithium excretion, raising lithium levels significantly — they are contraindicated.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats 'I should limit salt in my diet to prevent water retention since lithium can cause edema.' as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in Low-sodium diet (A) increases lithium retention and toxicity risk — the opposite of safe. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is 'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea, because delaying renal protection can worsen renal injury.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward 'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.', when the bedside priority is driven by Lithium safety: maintain consistent sodium/fluid intake. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is 'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on 'Ibuprofen is safe to take for headaches while on lithium.', while the decisive cue is Low-sodium diet (A) increases lithium retention and toxicity risk — the opposite of safe. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is 'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea, because delaying renal protection can worsen renal injury.

questions.takeaway: Lithium safety: maintain consistent sodium/fluid intake; avoid NSAIDs (raise lithium levels); report vomiting/diarrhea/excessive sweating immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient who delivered 2 hours ago reports sudden, severe perineal pain and pressure. The nurse inspects the perineum and notes a 4 cm tense, purple, fluctuant mass on the right labia. What does this indicate, and what is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Normal postpartum edema; apply ice pack and reassure the patient
Vulvar hematoma; notify the provider immediately and monitor vital signs
Episiotomy dehiscence; apply sterile dressing and call the surgeon
Bartholin's cyst; apply warm compresses

questions.rationale: A tense, purple, fluctuant (fluid-filled), rapidly expanding perineal mass with severe pain is a vulvar hematoma — accumulation of blood in soft tissue from injured blood vessels, often after vaginal delivery or episiotomy. Small hematomas (<3–4 cm) may be managed conservatively with ice and observation; larger or expanding hematomas require surgical incision and evacuation. The immediate nursing priority is to notify the provider, monitor vital signs for hemodynamic compromise, and apply an ice pack for comfort. Normal edema (A) is soft, not fluctuant or purple. Episiotomy dehiscence (C) would show wound separation. A Bartholin's cyst (D) is not a postpartum complication.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats Normal postpartum edema; apply ice pack and reassure the patient as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in what is the priority nursing action. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The nurse should prioritize Vulvar hematoma; notify the provider immediately and monitor vital signs; otherwise bleeding or anticoagulation injury remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Episiotomy dehiscence; apply sterile dressing and call the surgeon, instead of responding to what is the priority nursing action. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so Choosing it can delay bleeding-risk intervention and leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Bartholin's cyst; apply warm compresses would not address the priority, even though the higher-risk finding is A patient who delivered 2 hours ago reports sudden, severe perineal pain and pressure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Vulvar hematoma; notify the provider immediately and monitor vital signs, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Perineal hematoma: tense, fluctuant, purple mass with severe pain post-delivery — notify provider immediately; monitor for expansion and hemodynamic instability.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with C. difficile (CDI) colitis. Which instruction is MOST important to give the nursing assistant assigned to this patient's room?

options.text: Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after all patient contact to prevent C. diff spread
Wear gloves and gown for all contact with the patient and environment, and wash hands with soap and water before leaving the room
Wear only gloves since C. diff is transmitted via droplets
Apply an N95 respirator before entering the room due to airborne risk

questions.rationale: C. difficile is transmitted via the fecal-oral route through hardy spores. Key isolation requirements: contact precautions (gloves AND gown for all room entry), and crucially, soap and water handwashing rather than alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR). C. diff spores are NOT killed by alcohol-based sanitizers — soap and water physically removes spores from hands. ABHR is not effective against C. diff. C. diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required. Gloves alone (without gown) are insufficient for contact precautions.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after all patient contact to prevent C. diff spread would be unsafe, rather than acting on diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Wear gloves and gown for all contact with the patient and environment, and wash hands, not a step that delays stabilization of infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Wear only gloves since C. diff is transmitted via droplets fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on diff is not airborne or droplet-transmitted, so N95s are not required. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Apply an N95 respirator before entering the room due to airborne risk the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Key isolation requirements: contact precautions (gloves AND gown for all room entry). Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Wear gloves and gown for all contact with the patient and environment, and wash hands better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: C. diff = contact precautions (gloves + gown) + soap and water handwashing. Alcohol hand sanitizer does NOT kill C. diff spores. This is one of the few situations where soap/water is explicitly superior to ABHR.

questions.stem: A patient presents with BP 218/130 mmHg, severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion. Fundoscopic exam shows papilledema. Urinalysis shows proteinuria and hematuria. Which condition is present and what is the correct blood pressure management approach?

options.text: Hypertensive urgency — oral labetalol and discharge home with follow-up
Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — lower MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour using IV antihypertensives, with careful monitoring for ischemia
Hypertensive emergency — lower BP rapidly to < 120/80 mmHg within 1 hour to prevent further end-organ damage
Severe hypertension without emergency — reassess BP after 30 minutes of rest before intervening

questions.rationale: This patient has hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — severely elevated BP with end-organ damage: neurological (headache, confusion, papilledema = hypertensive encephalopathy), renal (proteinuria, hematuria). This requires IV antihypertensives (labetalol, nicardipine, or clevidipine) with arterial line monitoring. The critical teaching point: do NOT lower BP too rapidly. The target is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour. Organs autoregulate to high-pressure environments — a rapid drop can cause ischemic stroke, MI, or renal infarction as perfusion pressure suddenly falls. Hypertensive urgency has severe elevation but NO end-organ damage — treated with oral agents over hours to days.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Hypertensive urgency — oral labetalol and discharge home with follow-up is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, but the stem cue is The target is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour. Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — lower MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Hypertensive emergency — lower BP rapidly to < 120/80 mmHg within 1 hour to prevent further, without accounting for Organs autoregulate to high-pressure environments — a rapid drop can cause ischemic stroke, MI, or renal infarction as. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension) — lower MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Severe hypertension without emergency — reassess BP after 30 minutes of rest before intervening is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, and misses that The target is to reduce MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for renal protection while renal injury progresses.

questions.takeaway: Hypertensive emergency: severe HTN + end-organ damage = IV antihypertensives, reduce MAP ≤ 25% in hour 1. Then 160/100 over 2-6h. Then normalize over 24-48h. Rapid BP lowering causes ischemia — the brain has autoregulated UP.

visualRationale.title: Cardiovascular / Hypertensive Emergency
visualRationale.caption: Hypertensive emergency: severe HTN + end-organ damage = IV antihypertensives, reduce MAP ≤ 25% in hour 1. Then 160/100 over 2-6h. Then normalize over 24-48h. Rapid BP lowering causes ischemia — the brain has autoregulated UP.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypertensive emergency: severe HTN + end-organ damage = IV antihypertensives, reduce MAP ≤ 25% in hour 1. Then 160/100 over 2-6h. Then normalize over 24-48h. Rapid BP lowering causes ischemia — the brain has autoregulated UP.
